title: postMetric allocates fresh object literals on every call, unlike the pre-allocated pattern in decode-handler
description: EncodeHandler.postMetric() creates two new objects per invocation: `{ type: 'metric', sessionId, metric: { name, value } }`. DecodeHandler solved the same problem by pre-allocating `_metricMsg` and `_metricInner` and mutating them in place before postMessage (safe because structured clone is synchronous). The encode side has no equivalent, so every metric emission (encode_time_to_first_byte_ms, encode_chunk_yield_total_ms, encode_output_bytes, plus 6 calls in postFinalMetrics, plus any onMetric calls from the codec) allocates garbage. Under chunked streaming this fires frequently.

title: Third performance.now() call on the progress first-pixel path when two timestamps are already in scope
description: In the 'progress' event arm, `t0` is captured before toTransferablePixels and `now` is captured immediately after (line ~371 in feedDecoder for the push EMA, but inside readDecoderEvents the pattern uses a local t0 and then `performance.now()` again for tToArray). At line 483, `msg.timeToFirstPixelMs = performance.now() - this.stageStartMs` issues a third call even though `tToArray` gave us a post-copy timestamp just lines above. Using the already-captured `tToArray` end-point (or a local `afterCopy` variable) would eliminate this call on the hot first-pixel path.

title: onPixels allocates a new entry object on every pixel chunk even for the common no-region path
description: Every call to `onPixels` allocates `{ chunk }` or `{ chunk, region }` to push into `pixelQueue`. For a typical full-frame encode with many tiles or many frames, this creates one object per chunk that is short-lived (dequeued in feedEncoder). The decode-handler avoids this by pushing raw ArrayBuffers into ChunkRing. Encode semantics require tracking the optional region alongside the chunk, but a flat parallel array for regions (populated only when non-null) or a pre-allocated entry pool would reduce GC pressure on the streaming encode hot path.

title: maybePostDrain calls performance.now() unconditionally on every invocation, even when the guard will short-circuit
description: In EncodeHandler.maybePostDrain(), `const now = performance.now()` (line 382) runs before the early-return guards (`if (!drainAllowed) return`). Decode-handler avoids this by accepting `now` as a parameter already captured from the post-push timestamp, so no extra call occurs when drain is not allowed. The encode-handler re-captures time then often returns immediately, wasting the call. Fix: pass the already-known pushStart+pushMs endpoint as a parameter, mirroring the decode-handler pattern.

title: No throughput or allocation benchmark for the decode/encode handler hot paths
description: The test suite covers correctness (messages forwarded, metrics posted, pause/resume, budget) but has no benchmark that measures allocation rate or message-post latency for the worker_drain / postMetric / progress hot paths. The pre-allocated _metricMsg/_drainMsg objects in decode-handler and the lack of equivalent in encode-handler cannot be validated by existing tests. A microbenchmark (e.g., push N chunks, assert < M GC cycles or measure postMessage wall time) would catch regressions when future changes touch these paths.

title: compactQueue called after every takeNextPixels even when no compaction threshold is met
description: takeNextPixels() unconditionally calls compactQueue() after each dequeue (line 303 on the non-null path, line 298 on the null/empty path). compactQueue() itself guards with threshold checks, so the wasted work is two comparisons per dequeue plus a function-call overhead. This mirrors the decode-handler approach but ChunkRing avoids this entirely with O(1) circular-buffer shift. For encode sessions pushing thousands of tiles, the per-tile compactQueue call overhead accumulates. Inlining the threshold check at the call site (call compactQueue only when pixelReadIndex crosses the 64-entry boundary) would halve the number of function calls.
